modules.default_title: Lipid Management — CV Risk & Statin Prescribing
modules.landing_intro: Clinical decision support for lipid management and cardiovascular risk reduction in primary care. Use this module when evaluating a new patient on lipid therapy, initiating statins, or reviewing a patient not at LDL goal. Content is based on the AHA/ACC PREVENT Calculator (2023) and the 2018 AHA/ACC Cholesterol Guidelines.
modules.checklist_section_label: Before you decide — verify all four

checklist.statement: ASCVD risk tier calculated using PREVENT Calculator
Statin indication addressed and documented
LDL target identified and compared to current value
Medication plan documented with next follow-up

green_zone.narrative_html: Risk tier calculated, statin indication addressed, LDL goal identified, and medication plan documented. Patient is either at goal or has an active intensification plan in place. Follow-up interval appropriate to risk tier.

escalation.statement: LDL ≥190 mg/dL regardless of calculated risk score
Not at LDL goal on maximally tolerated statin
Borderline/intermediate risk patient is statin-hesitant
Patient meets criteria for Very High Risk reclassification
Advanced lipid markers indicated (ApoB, Lp(a))

context_strip.label: Organizational Standard
context_strip.text: AHA/ACC PREVENT Calculator is the required organizational risk stratification tool, replacing Pooled Cohort Equations. Epic BPA integration targeting October/November 2026. Until live, calculate manually at acc.org/PREVENT. SUPD (Statin Use in Persons with Diabetes) is a tracked VBC metric — HEDIS benchmark ~90%. All diabetic patients must have statin therapy addressed and documented at each visit.

modules.footer_note: Based on CV & Lipid Management Protocol (Draft, May 2026), Dr. Scott Hines MD — RBE East, Value-Based & Clinical Standards. ApoB, Lp(a), and CAC coverage confirmation pending — verify with payer before ordering. Not a substitute for clinical judgment. Module version 1.0.

faqs.title: Calculating ASCVD risk with the PREVENT Calculator

items.question: Why is PREVENT replacing the Pooled Cohort Equations?
items.answer_html: PREVENT incorporates kidney function (eGFR) and metabolic factors, and removes race as a variable — reducing known overestimation in certain populations. It is the 2023 AHA/ACC standard and Crystal Run's required organizational tool.

items.question: What are the four risk tiers and their thresholds?
items.answer_html: Low: <5% — no statin indicated. Borderline: 5–7.5% — shared decision-making; statin reasonable if LDL ≥70 plus risk enhancers. Intermediate: 7.5–20% — initiate statin. High: >20% — high-intensity statin, no shared decision-making required.

items.question: What are risk-enhancing factors for borderline-risk patients?
items.answer_html: Family history of premature ASCVD, hs-CRP ≥2 mg/L, ABI <0.9, elevated Lp(a) or ApoB. Presence of any of these at borderline risk (5–7.5%) with LDL ≥70 mg/dL supports statin initiation.

faqs.title: When and how to initiate statin therapy

items.question: When is high-intensity statin indicated without shared decision-making?
items.answer_html: Two automatic indications: (1) 10-year risk >20%, or (2) LDL ≥190 mg/dL regardless of risk score. Both are standard of care — document and initiate without requiring patient agreement process beyond standard informed consent.

items.question: When is shared decision-making required?
items.answer_html: All borderline-risk patients (5–7.5%). Document the conversation, the patient's preference, and the clinical factors considered. CAC score may support or defer initiation — CAC of 0 supports deferral; CAC >100 or ≥75th percentile supports initiation.

items.question: What is the SUPD requirement for diabetic patients?
items.answer_html: Statin Use in Persons with Diabetes is a HEDIS-tracked VBC metric with an organizational benchmark of ~90%. Every diabetic patient must have statin therapy addressed and documented at each visit, even if the patient declines or is intolerant.

faqs.title: LDL goals by risk tier

items.question: What are the LDL targets by tier?
items.answer_html: Very High Risk: <55 mg/dL. High Risk: <70 mg/dL. Intermediate Risk: <70–100 mg/dL depending on risk-enhancing factors. Borderline Risk: Shared decision-making — statin reasonable when LDL ≥70 plus risk enhancers.

items.question: What defines Very High Risk?
items.answer_html: Established ASCVD plus a second major cardiovascular event, OR one prior ASCVD event plus any of the following: diabetes, CKD, poorly controlled hypertension, current smoking, or LDL ≥100 mg/dL on maximally tolerated statin. Note: prior MI alone is High Risk; any comorbidity escalates to Very High Risk.

faqs.title: Documenting the medication plan and follow-up

items.question: What must be included in the medication plan documentation?
items.answer_html: Current statin name and intensity (low / moderate / high), whether patient is at LDL goal, next step if not at goal (intensify statin, add ezetimibe, or refer), and planned follow-up interval. Fasting lipid panel at 4–12 weeks after any change is standard.

items.question: What statin intensity classifications are used?
items.answer_html: High-intensity: Rosuvastatin 20–40 mg, Atorvastatin 40–80 mg. Moderate-intensity: Rosuvastatin 5–10 mg, Atorvastatin 10–20 mg, Simvastatin 20–40 mg. Low-intensity: Simvastatin 10 mg, Pravastatin 10–20 mg — generally insufficient for high or very high risk patients.

faqs.title: Managing severely elevated LDL

items.question: What action is required for LDL ≥190 mg/dL?
items.answer_html: Initiate high-intensity statin immediately — no shared decision-making required, no risk score threshold applies. Consider evaluation for familial hypercholesterolemia (FH). If LDL remains ≥100 mg/dL on maximally tolerated statin, add ezetimibe and consider PCSK9 inhibitor referral to cardiology.

faqs.title: Escalation pathway when LDL goal is not met

items.question: What is the escalation sequence when a patient is not at LDL goal?
items.answer_html: Step 1: Confirm adherence and maximize statin dose. Step 2: Add ezetimibe 10 mg daily — proven LDL reduction of 15–20%. Step 3: If still not at goal, refer to cardiology for PCSK9 inhibitor consideration (alirocumab, evolocumab). PCSK9 inhibitors require prior authorization and documented statin intolerance or failure.

faqs.title: Using CAC scoring to guide shared decision-making

items.question: When is CAC scoring appropriate?
items.answer_html: Ages 40–75 with borderline to intermediate risk (5–20%) who are hesitant about statin initiation. CAC of 0 supports deferral with reassessment in 5–7 years. CAC >100 or ≥75th percentile for age/sex supports initiation. Document the CAC result and the patient's decision in the chart.

items.question: Is CAC covered by insurance?
items.answer_html: Coverage confirmation is pending with the payer team. Verify before ordering. Cost is typically $75–150 out of pocket when not covered.

faqs.title: When to escalate from High to Very High Risk

items.question: What triggers Very High Risk reclassification?
items.answer_html: Established ASCVD plus a second major event, OR any prior ASCVD event combined with: diabetes, CKD, poorly controlled hypertension, current smoking, or LDL ≥100 mg/dL on maximally tolerated statin. Reclassification changes LDL target from <70 to <55 mg/dL and makes PCSK9 inhibitor referral appropriate if goal is not met.

items.question: Is cardiology referral required for Very High Risk patients?
items.answer_html: Not automatically required, but strongly consider referral when LDL goal (<55 mg/dL) is not being met on maximally tolerated statin plus ezetimibe.

faqs.title: ApoB and Lp(a) — when and how to order

items.question: When are ApoB and Lp(a) indicated?
items.answer_html: ApoB: Indicated for High and Very High Risk patients to assess residual risk beyond LDL, particularly when LDL appears controlled but cardiovascular risk remains unclear. Lp(a): Indicated once per lifetime for all patients with premature ASCVD, strong family history, or LDL ≥190 not fully explained by diet/secondary causes.

items.question: Is ordering ApoB or Lp(a) currently approved?
items.answer_html: Coverage and indications are pending confirmation with the payer team. Do not embed in order sets or SmartSets until confirmed. Manual ordering with documented clinical indication is acceptable in the interim.

references: Khan SS, et al. Novel Prediction Equations for Absolute Risk Assessment of Total Cardiovascular Disease Incorporating Cardiovascular-Kidney-Metabolic Health. Circulation. 2023;148(24):1982–2004.
Grundy SM, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. JACC. 2019;73(24):e285–e350.
Goff DC Jr, et al. 2013 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Assessment of Cardiovascular Risk. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2014;63(25 Pt B):2935–2959.
2022 ACC Expert Consensus Decision Pathway on Novel Therapies for Cardiovascular Risk Reduction.
European Atherosclerosis Society Lp(a) Consensus Statement, 2022.
Budoff MJ, et al. Coronary Artery Calcium: ACC/AHA Position Statement. JACC. 2018.
NCQA HEDIS Measures — updated annually at ncqa.org.

modules.landing_intro: A patient has an abnormal creatinine, or you've ordered cystatin C and the eGFR came back lower than expected. Use this checklist to complete a reasonable initial workup before escalating. Nephrology should receive a patient you've already started working — not one you're handing off cold.
modules.checklist_section_label: Before you escalate — complete all four

checklist.statement: Review current medication list for nephrotoxins and renally-cleared drugs requiring dose adjustment
Establish baseline: order cystatin C if not yet done, BMP, CBC, urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (uACR), urinalysis with microscopy
Stage the CKD using cystatin C-based eGFR (CKD-EPI 2021) and albuminuria category — document G and A stage
Blood pressure at goal for CKD (< 120/80 if tolerated) — document current readings and agent

green_zone.zone_label: All boxes checked — You've done the work
green_zone.narrative_html: Initial workup complete. Document staging and baseline using SmartPhrase below. Safe to manage stage G1–G3a with standard interventions while awaiting nephrology input if needed. You are institutionally supported.

modules.escalation_section_label: Escalate to nephrology e-consult if any apply

escalation.statement: eGFR decline > 5 mL/min/1.73m² in 12 months, or > 10 in 24 months
eGFR < 30 (G4–G5) at any point
uACR > 300 mg/g (severely increased albuminuria)
Unexplained CKD — no clear etiology after initial workup (no diabetes, no hypertension, no obstructive history)
Persistent hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs on microscopy

modules.footer_note: For internal use only. This is a viable first pathway, not the only pathway. Experienced providers retain clinical discretion.

faqs.title: Nephrotoxins and dose-adjusted drugs — what to look for

items.question: What medications should I flag immediately?
items.answer_html:
High-priority nephrotoxins to review in any CKD patient:

NSAIDs (including OTC ibuprofen/naproxen) — counsel to discontinue.

Metformin — generally safe to eGFR 30, reduce monitoring frequency below 45, hold below 30.

SGLT2 inhibitors — reassess below eGFR 45 (varies by agent and indication).

ACE inhibitors / ARBs — do not discontinue for mild creatinine bump (up to 30% rise is acceptable and expected); do reassess if rise exceeds that threshold.

Gabapentin/pregabalin — dose reduction required below eGFR 60.

Renally-cleared antibiotics — flag for any acute prescribing: nitrofurantoin contraindicated below eGFR 45.

items.question: My patient is on an NSAID for chronic pain. Do I stop it today?
items.answer_html:
In CKD G3a and above (eGFR < 60), the recommendation is to discontinue chronic NSAID use. Have a direct conversation: frame it as protecting kidney function, not removing pain management.

Offer alternatives — acetaminophen, topical diclofenac for localized pain, or a warm handoff to pain management if the underlying condition warrants it. Document the conversation and the plan.

items.question: Should I adjust doses myself or wait for nephrology?
items.answer_html:
For common medications with well-established renal dosing (metformin, gabapentin, many antibiotics), adjust now using the eGFR-based thresholds above. Do not wait for nephrology to make changes that are your responsibility as the PCP.

The e-consult is for guidance on management strategy — not for permission to stop nephrotoxins.

faqs.title: What to order and why — the CKD baseline panel

items.question: Why cystatin C if I already have a creatinine-based eGFR?
items.answer_html:
Creatinine-based eGFR is affected by muscle mass, diet, and race — it systematically overestimates GFR in patients with low muscle mass (elderly, frail, malnourished) and can underestimate in bodybuilders.

Cystatin C is filtered at a constant rate regardless of these variables. The CKD-EPI 2021 equation using both markers is now the preferred standard. When cystatin C eGFR is meaningfully lower than creatinine eGFR, believe cystatin C — it's the more accurate number.

items.question: My patient's cystatin C eGFR came back 15 points lower than their creatinine eGFR. Is that real?
items.answer_html:
Often yes. This is the most common anxiety point with cystatin C adoption. The lower number is not an error — it's a more accurate reflection of true GFR, particularly in older or leaner patients.

Stage and manage based on the cystatin C result. If this moves the patient into a higher-risk category, that's exactly the information you needed.

items.question: What does the uACR add?
items.answer_html:
Albuminuria is an independent risk factor for CKD progression and cardiovascular disease, separate from eGFR. A patient can have eGFR 65 with uACR > 300 and be at higher risk than a patient with eGFR 52 and uACR < 30.

Staging requires both dimensions — the G stage (eGFR) and the A stage (albuminuria). You cannot complete a CKD staging without it.

items.question: Does the urinalysis with microscopy need to be a fresh catch?
items.answer_html:
Ideally yes — a fresh midstream catch within 2 hours of collection gives the most reliable microscopy, particularly for RBC morphology. If the patient is in office, collect it that day.

If ordering remotely, instruct accordingly and note in the order.

faqs.title: Staging with cystatin C eGFR — what the numbers mean

items.question: What are the GFR stages and what do they mean clinically?
items.answer_html:
Using CKD-EPI 2021 cystatin C-based eGFR:

G1: ≥ 90 — Normal or high. CKD diagnosis requires other markers (albuminuria, structural abnormality). Monitor.

G2: 60–89 — Mildly decreased. Monitor annually if stable.

G3a: 45–59 — Mildly to moderately decreased. Begin medication review, BP optimization, RAAS therapy if proteinuric.

G3b: 30–44 — Moderately to severely decreased. Nephrology e-consult appropriate. Begin anemia workup, mineral/bone disease labs.

G4: 15–29 — Severely decreased. Nephrology referral (not just e-consult). RRT planning discussion begins.

G5: < 15 — Kidney failure. Active nephrology co-management required.

items.question: My patient is G3a. What do I actually do at this visit?
items.answer_html:
Three things: (1) Start or optimize an ACE inhibitor or ARB if they have albuminuria or diabetes — this is the single highest-impact intervention in early CKD. (2) Get BP below 120/80 if tolerated. (3) Counsel on nephrotoxin avoidance and dietary sodium restriction.

An SGLT2 inhibitor is indicated if they have diabetes or heart failure — this is now a standard of care intervention, not a specialist decision.

items.question: Do I need to tell the patient they have CKD?
items.answer_html:
Yes — this is a diagnosis, and it should be documented and communicated as such. Many patients with CKD G3 have been managed for years without anyone saying the words. Naming it clearly allows the patient to understand the stakes, engage in self-management, and make informed decisions.

Use plain language: "Your kidneys are working at about X% of normal. This is a condition called chronic kidney disease. It's manageable and we caught it at a stage where we can do a lot to protect them."

faqs.title: BP targets in CKD — tighter than you might think

items.question: Is 130/80 good enough?
items.answer_html:
Current KDIGO 2021 guidelines recommend a target of < 120 mmHg systolic if tolerated, based on SPRINT trial data. This is a meaningful shift from the older 130/80 standard.

In practice, tolerability is the limiting factor — orthostatic symptoms, syncope risk in elderly patients, and baseline hypotension all require individualization. Document your target and your rationale.

items.question: Which agents are preferred?
items.answer_html:
ACE inhibitor or ARB is first-line in CKD with albuminuria (uACR > 30) or diabetes, regardless of BP. Do not use both together — dual RAAS blockade increases harm without added benefit.

For additional BP control: dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (amlodipine) and thiazide-like diuretics (chlorthalidone preferred over HCTZ in CKD) are appropriate add-ons. Loop diuretics for volume management in G4–G5.

items.question: My patient's creatinine bumped after I started the ACE inhibitor. Should I stop it?
items.answer_html:
Not automatically. A rise in creatinine of up to 30% after initiating an ACE inhibitor or ARB is expected, acceptable, and does not indicate harm — it reflects the intended hemodynamic effect on the glomerulus.

Recheck in 2 weeks. If the rise is greater than 30% or potassium rises above 5.5, then hold and reassess. If you stop every ACE inhibitor that causes any creatinine bump, you will deprive CKD patients of their most protective therapy.

faqs.title: eGFR declining faster than expected — what to do

items.question: How do I know if the decline is rapid?
items.answer_html:
Pull prior creatinine values from the chart — at minimum 12 months back, ideally 24. Calculate the trajectory. A decline of more than 5 mL/min/1.73m² per year is considered rapid and warrants e-consult regardless of current stage.

A single low value without prior data is not the same as demonstrated progression — establish the trend first.

items.question: Could something acute be driving this?
items.answer_html:
Yes — always rule out AKI superimposed on CKD before concluding progression. Recent illness, volume depletion, new nephrotoxin exposure, or urinary obstruction can all cause acute drops.

If there's any question, recheck in 4–6 weeks after addressing potential acute factors before framing as progression.

faqs.title: eGFR < 30 — escalation and preparation

items.question: What changes at G4?
items.answer_html:
Several things happen simultaneously at G4 that go beyond standard PCP management:

(1) Nephrology referral (not just e-consult) is indicated. (2) Mineral and bone disease workup: PTH, phosphorus, 25-OH vitamin D, calcium. (3) Anemia of CKD evaluation. (4) RRT planning conversation — not to alarm, but to inform. Patients who arrive at dialysis having never discussed it are poorly prepared.

items.question: Should I be starting erythropoiesis-stimulating agents?
items.answer_html:
ESA initiation in CKD is a nephrology decision. Your role at G4 is to identify anemia, complete the workup (iron studies, B12, folate, retic count), correct iron deficiency if present, and document.

The e-consult to nephrology should include this workup so they can advise on ESA candidacy with full information in hand.

faqs.title: Severely increased albuminuria — uACR > 300

items.question: Is this nephrotic range?
items.answer_html:
Not necessarily — nephrotic range proteinuria is traditionally defined as > 3.5 g/day on 24-hour urine, or roughly uACR > 2000–3500 mg/g. A uACR of 300–2000 is severely increased but not nephrotic.

However, any uACR > 300 warrants e-consult regardless of eGFR, as it indicates significant glomerular pathology and accelerated progression risk.

items.question: Should I repeat it before acting?
items.answer_html:
A single elevated uACR should be confirmed on a second sample (ideally first morning void) before staging — transient elevations occur with fever, exercise, and UTI.

However, if the value is markedly elevated (> 300) and there's no obvious transient cause, begin the workup in parallel with confirmation rather than waiting.

faqs.title: CKD without a clear cause

items.question: My patient has CKD but no diabetes, no hypertension, no obstructive history. What now?
items.answer_html:
This is the e-consult indication — unexplained CKD requires nephrology input to consider glomerulonephritis, genetic nephropathy, or other primary renal disease.

Before the consult, complete: urinalysis with microscopy (looking for casts), uACR, renal ultrasound, serum protein electrophoresis in patients > 40 (to screen for myeloma), ANA and complement if clinical picture suggests autoimmune.

Document what you've ruled out — the nephrologist can then focus on what remains.

faqs.title: Persistent hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs

items.question: What do dysmorphic RBCs mean?
items.answer_html:
Dysmorphic RBCs — particularly acanthocytes — on urine microscopy indicate glomerular origin of bleeding, as opposed to urothelial sources (stone, tumor, infection). This is a nephrology finding, not a urology finding.

If the microscopy report specifically mentions dysmorphic RBCs or acanthocytes, the e-consult goes to nephrology, not to urology for cystoscopy.

items.question: What if the report just says "RBCs present" without commenting on morphology?
items.answer_html:
Standard urinalysis does not assess morphology — that requires microscopy with a trained eye. If you have > 5 RBCs/hpf on two separate specimens without an obvious benign cause, request formal microscopy.

In many practices this requires a specific order or a call to the lab.

modules.landing_intro: A new or transitioning patient is on stimulant medication. Use this checklist to determine whether to continue, document, or escalate. Tap any item for guidance.
modules.checklist_section_label: Before you decide — verify all four

checklist.statement: PDMP reviewed — no concerning pattern
No active substance use disorder (or documented awareness & plan)
Medication appears effective — patient functional, no recent dose escalation requests
No early fill requests in the past 12 months

green_zone.narrative_html: Continue current regimen. Document using SmartPhrase below. You are institutionally supported.

modules.escalation_section_label: Escalate to psychiatry e-consult if any apply

escalation.statement: Diagnosis based on self-report alone — no prior formal workup documented
Dose at or near ceiling, or recently escalated without documented clinical rationale
Functional decline or behavioral red flags despite medication
Patient requesting combination stimulants or multi-agent regimen

context_strip.label: National context
context_strip.text: Primary care providers across the US manage a meaningful proportion of adult ADHD stimulant prescriptions. You are not an outlier for inheriting these patients. The question is not whether to manage — it is how to manage safely.

modules.footer_note: This is a viable first pathway, not the only pathway. Experienced providers retain clinical discretion. This framework provides institutional backing when followed.

faqs.title: What counts as a "concerning pattern"?

items.question: How often should I check the PDMP?
items.answer_html:
At minimum, check at the first appointment with a new-to-you patient and at every controlled substance renewal thereafter. Quarterly checks at prescription renewal visits are a reasonable standard for stable patients.

New York State requires PDMP review prior to prescribing controlled substances. This is a legal obligation, not a preference.

items.question: What specifically am I looking for?
items.answer_html:
Red flags that warrant pause before prescribing:

Multiple prescribers — stimulants from more than one provider in the past 12 months without clear documented coordination.

Multiple pharmacies — controlled substances filled at 3+ pharmacies without a clear geographic or insurance reason.

Concurrent opiates — stimulants plus opioids without documented pain management rationale.

Dose mismatch — quantity dispensed doesn't match what any single prescriber would authorize (suggests supplemental sourcing).

Reassuring pattern — same prescriber, same pharmacy, consistent quantity, no gaps suggesting diversion, no overlapping controlled substances.

items.question: I see something concerning. What do I do?
items.answer_html:
Do not prescribe at this visit. Have a direct, non-accusatory conversation with the patient: "I'm seeing some things in the prescription history I want to understand before we go further."

Document exactly what you found and what the patient told you. This is protective for you and the institution regardless of what you decide next.

If the concern is significant, this is an appropriate trigger for an e-consult to psychiatry with the specific question of safe prescribing plan rather than routine review.

items.question: The prior provider didn't document any PDMP checks. Does that mean I'm starting over?
items.answer_html:
Yes — when you become the prescriber of record, the documentation obligation transfers to you. The prior provider's omission is their liability, not yours, but it does mean your first check is essential before issuing your first prescription.

Think of it as a brief triage at the first handoff appointment. It takes under two minutes and is your best protection.

faqs.title: What if there's a substance use disorder in the history?

items.question: The patient has a past SUD. Is that a dealbreaker?
items.answer_html:
Not automatically — but it shifts the burden of documentation significantly. A patient in sustained, well-documented remission who has been stable on stimulants for years is a different clinical picture than a patient with active use.

The key question: is the current prescription part of a coordinated plan, or did it evolve informally without the SUD history being factored in?

If there's no documented awareness of the SUD history in the prior prescribing record, this is a case for psychiatry e-consult — not to decide whether to continue, but to have a documented expert review in the chart.

items.question: What does "documented awareness and plan" look like?
items.answer_html:
At minimum: a note that the provider is aware of the SUD history, has considered it in the prescribing decision, and has a monitoring plan. This can be brief.

Example language: "Patient has history of [SUD], currently in remission x [timeframe]. Stimulant therapy reviewed in this context. Continued with close monitoring including PDMP review at each renewal and urine drug screen [frequency]."

SmartPhrase .CSADHD-SUD-AWARE covers this documentation.

items.question: The patient currently uses cannabis. Does that count?
items.answer_html:
Cannabis use alone does not automatically disqualify a patient from stimulant continuation, but it should be documented and acknowledged. The concern is whether cannabis use represents a broader pattern of substance use that increases misuse risk.

Heavy or problematic cannabis use warrants the same documentation escalation as any other SUD history. Occasional use in a stable, well-functioning patient is a clinical judgment call — document your reasoning either way.

faqs.title: How do I assess whether the medication is actually working?

items.question: I'm inheriting the patient — I never saw them before treatment. How can I judge efficacy?
items.answer_html:
You're assessing current functional status, not comparing to a baseline you never witnessed. Ask directly: "How are things going at work / school / home? Are you managing the things you were struggling with before medication?"

If the patient can describe specific, credible improvements in function — and there's no evidence of dose escalation, misuse, or new concerns — that's a reasonable basis for continuation.

You're not re-validating the original diagnosis. You're verifying that an ongoing prescription is doing what it's supposed to do.

items.question: The patient says it's working but I'm not sure I believe them.
items.answer_html:
This is a clinical judgment call that doesn't have a bright-line answer. A few considerations:

Is there corroboration? Prior notes from a previous provider, a therapist, a partner or employer? Even a prior rating scale in the chart?

Are there behavioral inconsistencies? Does the patient's presentation, history, or PDMP pattern suggest misuse or diversion rather than treatment?

If your clinical instinct is firing and you can't articulate why you're comfortable, that's the moment to pause, document, and consider an e-consult. That consult is not punitive — it's you doing exactly what the framework asks.

items.question: Should I be using a rating scale at follow-up visits?
items.answer_html:
Formal rating scales (Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale, Conners Adult ADHD Rating Scale) are better suited to initial evaluation and are not expected at every follow-up for established patients.

Brief structured questions — functional domains, side effects, adherence — are sufficient and documentable for ongoing management. The goal is a consistent, reproducible assessment, not a diagnostic workup at every visit.

faqs.title: Early fill requests — what's the threshold?

items.question: How early is "too early"?
items.answer_html:
A single request more than 5 days early is worth a conversation. A pattern of requests more than 5 days early across multiple fill cycles is a red flag warranting documentation and possible escalation.

Context matters: a patient who ran out early once because of a vacation schedule is different from a patient who consistently fills 10 days early over 12 months.

Pattern: 2+ early fills in 12 months — document, address directly with patient, consider urine drug screen.

items.question: Can I fill early for travel or vacation?
items.answer_html:
New York State Schedule II controlled substances (most stimulants) cannot be post-dated or filled early beyond the state dispensing window, regardless of reason. Patients traveling internationally should plan ahead with their prescribing provider well in advance — this is not something that can be resolved at the last minute.

For domestic travel, most pharmacies can fill within a few days of the allowed window. Counsel patients to plan accordingly.

items.question: The patient says they lost the medication. What do I do?
items.answer_html:
Lost or stolen Schedule II controlled substances are not replaced as a matter of policy. This is both a regulatory constraint and a risk management position.

Communicate this clearly and without apology: "I understand this is frustrating. Unfortunately, Schedule II medications can't be replaced in these circumstances — this is a regulatory requirement, not a decision I'm making about you personally."

Document the report, check the PDMP, and note the conversation. A single loss event is not necessarily disqualifying for continued prescribing, but it should be in the chart.

faqs.title: The prior diagnosis looks thin. Now what?

items.question: The chart has an ADHD diagnosis but no workup. Am I on the hook for that?
items.answer_html:
You are not responsible for the prior diagnostic process, but you are responsible for decisions made from this point forward. Continuing a prescription is a clinical decision — meaning it's yours, and it should be defensible.

The question isn't "was this diagnosis legitimate?" It's "do I have enough information to comfortably continue this medication?" If the answer is no, the e-consult pathway exists precisely for this situation.

items.question: What does a "formal workup" actually mean? Is neuropsychological testing required?
items.answer_html:
Neuropsychological testing is not required for an ADHD diagnosis and is not the standard of care in most clinical settings. A reasonable workup includes:

A validated rating scale (e.g., Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale) completed by the patient. A structured clinical interview addressing symptom onset, duration, and functional impairment across settings. Consideration of differential diagnoses — anxiety, mood disorders, sleep disorders, and thyroid disease can all mimic ADHD.

If none of this is in the chart, that's thin — but it's a very common chart. The e-consult purpose here is to get a psychiatrist's eyes on the case so you have expert documentation in the record. It changes the risk profile for both of you.

items.question: The patient is stable, functional, and has been on the medication for 5 years. Does the shaky diagnosis still matter?
items.answer_html:
Clinically, a 5-year stable course with demonstrated functional benefit is meaningful evidence in its own right. The medication is clearly doing something for this person.

That said, "doing something" doesn't always mean treating ADHD — stimulants improve focus and executive function across many conditions and in some people without a formal diagnosis. The diagnostic question remains open.

For practical purposes: a long-stable patient with no red flags and a thin chart is a lower priority for formal re-evaluation than a recent diagnosis, a request to escalate dose, or emerging behavioral concerns. Use clinical judgment on triage order.

faqs.title: Ceiling doses and recent escalations

items.question: What are the ceiling doses for common stimulants?
items.answer_html:
Amphetamine salts (Adderall) — FDA max 60 mg/day for adults. Doses above 40 mg/day in primary care warrant documentation of rationale.

Methylphenidate (Ritalin, Concerta) — FDA max 60 mg/day. Extended-release formulations vary by product; check prescribing information.

Lisdexamfetamine (Vyvanse) — FDA max 70 mg/day. This is a hard ceiling due to the prodrug formulation.

Doses above FDA maximum are outside this framework and require specialist co-management.

items.question: The patient was escalated to max dose by the prior provider. Do I continue?
items.answer_html:
Continuing a maximum-dose prescription is within primary care scope if it's clinically justified and documented. The burden of documentation is higher at the ceiling than at moderate doses.

At first visit: review the chart for the rationale behind escalation. If it's documented and coherent — continue with your own documented review.

If there's no documented rationale for the ceiling dose — or if the escalation happened recently and rapidly — this is an e-consult trigger. The question for psychiatry: "Patient is at maximum dose of [medication] without clear documented rationale. Please review current plan and advise whether to maintain, taper, or restructure."

faqs.title: When function declines despite medication

items.question: What counts as a "behavioral red flag"?
items.answer_html:
Behavioral red flags in this context are signs that the medication may be causing harm, being misused, or masking an underlying condition that's worsening:

Mood instability — new or worsening irritability, emotional dysregulation, or rage that the patient or family attributes to medication timing.

Sleep severe enough to impair function — beyond expected stimulant effect, especially if the patient is escalating to cope.

Cardiovascular symptoms — palpitations, chest pain, or significant blood pressure elevation at current dose.

Increasing psychological dependence — patient describes inability to function at all without the medication in ways that feel qualitatively different from therapeutic benefit.

items.question: Could this be something other than ADHD at this point?
items.answer_html:
Yes — and this is one of Dr. Batson's core concerns about the diagnostic rigor of many adult ADHD diagnoses. Anxiety disorders, bipolar disorder (particularly type II), and PTSD all significantly overlap with ADHD symptom profiles.

Stimulants can worsen anxiety, destabilize mood in bipolar disorder, and in some cases worsen trauma-related hyperarousal. If a patient is deteriorating on stimulants, these are worth considering.

This doesn't mean stopping the medication unilaterally — it means this patient needs psychiatric input, and the e-consult should frame that question explicitly.

faqs.title: Patient requesting combination or second stimulant

items.question: Is combination stimulant therapy ever appropriate?
items.answer_html:
Combination stimulant therapy (e.g., long-acting amphetamine plus short-acting methylphenidate) exists in clinical practice, particularly in severe ADHD managed by specialists. It is outside the scope of primary care initiation and requires specialist documentation.

If a patient arrives already on a multi-agent regimen, this is a situation where the e-consult precedes any continuation decision — not to refuse, but to have expert sign-off in the chart before you own this prescription.

items.question: The patient is asking me to add a booster dose to their existing medication.
items.answer_html:
Adding a short-acting "booster" to a long-acting regimen is common in specialist practice and not inherently inappropriate. But initiating this in primary care without specialist guidance or documentation is high-exposure territory.

The conversation with the patient: "That's a reasonable clinical question. I want to make sure we approach it the right way, which means getting a psychiatry review before we add anything new to your regimen. I'm not saying no — I'm saying let's do this properly."

This is precisely the e-consult scenario: patient-requested escalation requiring specialist input before action.

items.question: The patient had a booster dose prescribed before and wants to restart it.
items.answer_html:
Restarting a previously prescribed regimen is lower risk than initiating something new, especially if it's documented in prior records. The PDMP will show whether this was previously dispensed.

If the prior prescribing record shows the combination was used, was safe, and was discontinued for a non-clinical reason (e.g., provider left the practice), continuation with documentation is defensible.

If you're not comfortable, the e-consult is always available. That's not a failure — that's the framework doing its job.

modules.landing_intro: A patient has a low hemoglobin. The reflex to immediately consult hematology is understandable — but a hematologist receiving a referral with only a CBC and no workup cannot do much from a distance. Your job is to complete a structured initial evaluation that answers the first-order question: what kind of anemia is this? Most anemia seen in primary care has a diagnosable and treatable cause that does not require hematology at all. For those that do, arriving with a completed workup transforms the consult from a hand-off into a collaboration.
modules.checklist_section_label: Before you escalate — complete all four

checklist.statement: CBC with differential reviewed — MCV categorized as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic
Reticulocyte count obtained — production vs. destruction vs. loss established
Iron studies obtained if microcytic or mixed — serum iron, TIBC, ferritin, transferrin saturation
B12 and folate obtained if macrocytic or normocytic with clinical suspicion

green_zone.zone_label: All boxes checked — You have a working diagnosis
green_zone.narrative_html: Initial workup complete. You can now categorize and begin treating the most common causes — iron deficiency, B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, or anemia of chronic disease — without hematology involvement. Document findings and plan using SmartPhrase below. Hematology e-consult is indicated only for unexplained, refractory, or complex findings. You are institutionally supported.

modules.escalation_section_label: Escalate to hematology e-consult if any apply

escalation.statement: Hemoglobin < 7 g/dL or symptomatic at any level (chest pain, dyspnea at rest, hemodynamic instability)
Workup complete — no etiology identified after iron studies, B12, folate, reticulocyte count, and clinical review
Evidence of hemolysis — elevated LDH, elevated indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin, elevated reticulocyte count without blood loss
Abnormal WBC or platelet count accompanying anemia — pancytopenia, blasts on differential, or thrombocytopenia without explanation
Iron deficiency anemia not responding to 3 months of adequate oral iron replacement
Anemia in setting of CKD G3b or above — see Meridian CKD module for coordinated workup

modules.footer_note: For internal use only. This is a viable first pathway, not the only pathway. Experienced providers retain clinical discretion. Urgent findings — schistocytes, blasts, severe symptomatic anemia — require same-day escalation outside this e-consult framework.

faqs.title: Reading the CBC — MCV as your first branch point

items.question: What MCV thresholds define the categories?
items.answer_html:
Standard laboratory reference ranges:

Microcytic: MCV < 80 fL — think iron deficiency first, then thalassemia, then anemia of chronic disease (can be microcytic), then sideroblastic anemia (rare).

Normocytic: MCV 80–100 fL — think anemia of chronic disease, early iron deficiency, renal anemia, hypothyroidism, mixed deficiency, or early bone marrow pathology.

Macrocytic: MCV > 100 fL — think B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, hypothyroidism, alcohol use, medications (methotrexate, hydroxyurea, zidovudine), or myelodysplastic syndrome in older patients.

MCV is your first branch point. Every subsequent test order flows from this categorization.

items.question: The MCV is normal but the hemoglobin is low. Does that mean it's anemia of chronic disease?
items.answer_html:
Normocytic anemia is the broadest category and requires the most differential thinking. Anemia of chronic disease is common but should be a diagnosis of context — meaning the patient has a known chronic inflammatory condition (rheumatoid arthritis, IBD, malignancy, CKD) that explains it.

Normocytic anemia without a clear chronic disease context requires reticulocyte count as the next step to distinguish between underproduction and loss or destruction.

items.question: The RDW is elevated. What does that tell me?
items.answer_html:
Elevated RDW (red cell distribution width) indicates anisocytosis — a mixed population of cell sizes. This is particularly useful in two scenarios:

(1) A microcytic anemia with high RDW suggests iron deficiency rather than thalassemia (thalassemia tends to have normal or low RDW despite low MCV).

(2) A normocytic anemia with high RDW may indicate a mixed deficiency — iron plus B12 or folate — where the two processes cancel each other out in terms of MCV while the RDW reflects the heterogeneity. When the MCV looks normal but the RDW is high, order both iron studies and B12/folate.

items.question: Should I be looking at the differential on every anemia CBC?
items.answer_html:
Yes — the differential provides information the MCV alone cannot. Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥ 5 lobes) are a classic finding in B12 or folate deficiency and may precede macrocytosis. Target cells suggest hemoglobinopathy or liver disease.

Schistocytes (fragmented RBCs) indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia — TTP, HUS, DIC — and are an emergency finding. If you see schistocytes on a differential, escalate immediately.

faqs.title: Production vs. destruction vs. loss — why this test matters

items.question: What does the reticulocyte count tell me that the CBC doesn't?
items.answer_html: The reticulocyte count tells you what the bone marrow is doing in response to the anemia. A normal bone marrow facing anemia should increase production — reticulocytes should rise. If they don't, the problem is in the bone marrow or in the substrate (iron, B12, folate). If they are elevated, the marrow is responding appropriately, which means the problem is peripheral — blood loss or destruction (hemolysis).

items.question: How do I interpret the reticulocyte count correctly?
items.answer_html:
Use the reticulocyte production index (RPI) rather than the raw reticulocyte percentage, which is misleading in anemia because it is expressed as a percentage of a smaller RBC pool:

RPI = (reticulocyte % × patient hematocrit) ÷ 45

RPI < 2: hypoproliferative — bone marrow is not responding appropriately. Think iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, anemia of chronic disease, renal anemia, or bone marrow pathology.

RPI > 2: hyperproliferative — marrow is responding. Think hemolysis or acute blood loss.

items.question: I ordered a reticulocyte count and it came back as a percentage only. Is that enough?
items.answer_html:
A raw percentage without the RPI calculation is limited — you need to interpret it in context. A reticulocyte percentage of 2.5% sounds elevated but may represent an RPI of 1.2 in a severely anemic patient, indicating an inadequate response.

Do the RPI calculation yourself using the formula above and document it in the note.

faqs.title: Iron deficiency — diagnosing and treating correctly

items.question: What is the most reliable test for iron deficiency?
items.answer_html:
Ferritin is the most sensitive single marker for iron deficiency when low — a ferritin < 30 ng/mL is highly specific for iron deficiency in most clinical contexts. However, ferritin is an acute phase reactant and is elevated by inflammation, infection, liver disease, and malignancy.

A patient with an inflammatory condition can have a ferritin of 80 ng/mL and still be iron deficient. In the context of chronic inflammation, a ferritin up to 100 ng/mL does not exclude iron deficiency — transferrin saturation < 20% in this context supports the diagnosis. Use the full panel: serum iron, TIBC, ferritin, and transferrin saturation together.

items.question: I've confirmed iron deficiency. How do I treat it?
items.answer_html:
Oral iron is first-line for most patients:

Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) every other day — as effective as daily dosing with significantly fewer GI side effects. This is a meaningful recent evidence update from the older twice-daily standard.

Take on an empty stomach with vitamin C (orange juice) to maximize absorption. Avoid within 2 hours of calcium, antacids, PPIs, or thyroid medication.

Recheck CBC and reticulocyte count in 4 weeks — you should see reticulocyte response within 1–2 weeks and hemoglobin rise of 1–2 g/dL by 4 weeks if the diagnosis and absorption are correct.

Continue treatment for 3 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replete stores.

items.question: My patient cannot tolerate oral iron or has a PPI she won't stop. What are my options?
items.answer_html:
IV iron is appropriate and effective for patients with malabsorption, intolerance to oral iron, inflammatory bowel disease, or who are on PPIs that cannot be discontinued. IV iron formulations (ferric carboxymaltose, low molecular weight iron dextran, ferumoxytol) can be administered in an infusion center.

This is within primary care scope to order — you do not need hematology referral to initiate IV iron in uncomplicated iron deficiency anemia.

items.question: I found iron deficiency. Do I need to find the source?
items.answer_html:
In most cases yes — iron deficiency in an adult is blood loss until proven otherwise. In premenopausal women with heavy menstrual bleeding, the source is often clinically apparent and colonoscopy is not mandatory in the absence of GI symptoms or red flags.

In men and postmenopausal women, iron deficiency requires GI evaluation — upper endoscopy and colonoscopy — to exclude occult GI blood loss including malignancy. Do not treat iron deficiency in a postmenopausal woman or any man without addressing the question of GI source.

items.question: Ferritin is 150 and transferrin saturation is 15%. Is this iron deficiency?
items.answer_html:
Possibly — this pattern (normal or elevated ferritin, low transferrin saturation) in the context of chronic inflammation is called functional iron deficiency or iron-restricted erythropoiesis. The iron exists in stores but is not being released for use. This is distinct from absolute iron deficiency and is the hallmark of anemia of chronic disease with iron restriction.

Treatment depends on the underlying condition — treating the inflammation is the primary intervention, supplemental iron has limited benefit in pure anemia of chronic disease, and IV iron may be considered in select cases (particularly CKD — see CKD module).

faqs.title: Macrocytic anemia — deficiencies and their distinctions

items.question: When should I order B12 and folate?
items.answer_html: Always order both when MCV > 100. Also consider ordering in: normocytic anemia with elevated RDW, hypersegmented neutrophils on differential, neurological symptoms (peripheral neuropathy, cognitive change, balance problems), history of gastric bypass or significant GI resection, strict vegan diet without supplementation, chronic PPI use (reduces B12 absorption over years), metformin use (reduces B12 absorption — check annually in long-term users), or active alcohol use disorder.

items.question: The B12 level is 250 pg/mL — normal range starts at 200. Is this enough to exclude deficiency?
items.answer_html:
No — serum B12 is an unreliable marker at the low-normal range. Levels between 200–400 pg/mL can be associated with functional deficiency, particularly in symptomatic patients.

If clinical suspicion is present, order methylmalonic acid (MMA) and homocysteine — both are elevated in B12 deficiency and are more sensitive markers of functional deficiency than serum B12 alone. A normal MMA effectively excludes B12 deficiency regardless of the serum level.

items.question: I've confirmed B12 deficiency. Do I need to find the cause before treating?
items.answer_html:
Treat first, investigate in parallel. B12 deficiency with neurological symptoms is a semi-urgent treatment situation — neurological damage from B12 deficiency can be permanent if prolonged. Initiate replacement immediately.

The most common cause in older adults is pernicious anemia (autoimmune gastritis with intrinsic factor deficiency) — check anti-intrinsic factor antibody and anti-parietal cell antibody. If pernicious anemia is confirmed, parenteral B12 (IM cyanocobalamin) is traditionally used, though high-dose oral B12 (1000–2000 mcg daily) is absorbed adequately via passive diffusion even without intrinsic factor and is now considered equivalent by most guidelines.

items.question: Folate is low. Is the workup the same as B12?
items.answer_html:
Folate deficiency is simpler in etiology — it is almost always dietary (poor intake, alcohol use disorder, malabsorption) or medication-related (methotrexate, trimethoprim, phenytoin). Unlike B12 deficiency, folate deficiency does not cause neurological symptoms — if a macrocytic anemia patient has neurological symptoms, look harder at B12 regardless of folate level.

Treatment is oral folic acid 1 mg daily. Identify and address the underlying cause — folate deficiency in a patient not on a causative medication should prompt dietary and alcohol use assessment.

items.question: My patient is on methotrexate and has macrocytic anemia. Is this just expected?
items.answer_html:
Methotrexate causes macrocytosis by folate antagonism — this is an expected pharmacological effect and does not necessarily indicate deficiency or require dose change. However, if the anemia is significant or the patient is symptomatic, check folate level and ensure they are on folic acid supplementation (standard with methotrexate therapy).

Coordinate with the prescribing provider before making changes to the methotrexate regimen.

faqs.title: When anemia is urgent — thresholds and immediate management

items.question: What are the symptoms that make any hemoglobin level urgent?
items.answer_html:
Chest pain, dyspnea at rest, tachycardia, hypotension, syncope, or altered mental status in the context of anemia require same-day evaluation regardless of the absolute hemoglobin level.

A patient with hemoglobin of 9 g/dL who is having chest pain and tachycardia is more urgent than a patient with hemoglobin of 6.5 g/dL who is ambulatory and asymptomatic. Symptoms drive urgency more than numbers.

items.question: At what hemoglobin level should I be considering transfusion?
items.answer_html:
Transfusion thresholds in stable outpatients:

Hemoglobin < 7 g/dL — transfusion is generally indicated in symptomatic patients. In asymptomatic, young, otherwise healthy patients with a clearly reversible cause (iron deficiency being actively treated), watchful waiting with close monitoring is sometimes appropriate — discuss with hematology.

Hemoglobin 7–8 g/dL — transfusion guided by symptoms and underlying condition. Patients with cardiac disease or limited cardiopulmonary reserve may need transfusion at higher thresholds.

Hemoglobin > 8 g/dL — transfusion rarely indicated in stable outpatients without active bleeding or severe symptoms.

Outpatient transfusion can be arranged through infusion centers — this does not require ER admission for a stable but significantly anemic patient.

items.question: The patient has hemoglobin of 6.8 and is mildly symptomatic on exertion. Do I send them to the ER?
items.answer_html:
Not necessarily — if the patient is stable, ambulatory, not having chest pain or tachycardia, and you can arrange outpatient transfusion within 24–48 hours, this is manageable without an ER visit. Call the infusion center, arrange the transfusion, and ensure the patient has clear return precautions.

If outpatient transfusion cannot be arranged promptly or the patient's condition is at all unstable, send to the ER with a direct communication to the receiving team.

faqs.title: Workup complete — still no answer

items.question: I've done the full initial workup and I can't explain the anemia. What have I potentially missed?
items.answer_html:
Review this list before the e-consult:

Hypothyroidism — TSH should be part of any unexplained normocytic or macrocytic anemia workup.

Renal insufficiency — check creatinine and eGFR; anemia of CKD is common and underrecognized.

Chronic liver disease — LFTs, consider hepatitis screening.

Lead toxicity — in patients with occupational exposure, recent immigration from high-exposure regions, or pica; order blood lead level.

Medication review — many drugs cause anemia through various mechanisms; review the full medication list.

Alcohol use — often underreported; ask directly.

Inflammatory markers — ESR, CRP, ferritin as acute phase reactant; anemia of chronic inflammation may not have an obvious clinical source.

items.question: What does the hematology e-consult ask look like for unexplained anemia?
items.answer_html:
Be specific and include your workup: "Patient with [degree] [MCV category] anemia, hemoglobin [X]. Workup completed: iron studies [results], B12 [result], folate [result], reticulocyte count/RPI [result], TSH [result], CMP [result]. No clear etiology identified. No evidence of hemolysis. CBC differential without abnormal forms. Requesting evaluation for underlying cause and management recommendation."

A hematologist receiving this consult can move directly to the next tier of evaluation rather than repeating your workup.

faqs.title: When the marrow is working but the cells are being destroyed

items.question: What lab pattern confirms hemolysis?
items.answer_html:
The hemolysis panel: elevated LDH (released from lysed RBCs), elevated indirect bilirubin (from heme catabolism), low or undetectable haptoglobin (binds free hemoglobin and is consumed), and elevated reticulocyte count/RPI (marrow responding to peripheral destruction).

Not all markers need to be abnormal simultaneously — low haptoglobin alone has high specificity for hemolysis. Order the full panel and document which markers are positive.

items.question: I have confirmed hemolysis. What are the common causes I should consider before hematology?
items.answer_html:
Categorize by mechanism:

Immune-mediated — warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA). Order direct antiglobulin test (DAT/Coombs). A positive DAT confirms immune-mediated destruction and is the most common cause of hemolysis in primary care patients. Associated with autoimmune conditions, CLL, and certain medications (methyldopa, penicillin, cephalosporins).

Microangiopathic — schistocytes on smear: TTP, HUS, DIC, malignant hypertension. This is an emergency — do not manage outpatient.

Hereditary — G6PD deficiency (often triggered by infection, oxidative drugs, fava beans — order G6PD level), hereditary spherocytosis (spherocytes on smear, family history), sickle cell disease (usually known).

items.question: I see schistocytes on the peripheral smear. What do I do?
items.answer_html:
Treat as urgent. Schistocytes indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia — RBCs being sheared by damaged small vessels. TTP (thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura) is life-threatening and time-sensitive — check platelet count immediately.

Thrombocytopenia plus microangiopathic hemolytic anemia is TTP until proven otherwise and requires same-day hematology contact, not an e-consult. Send the patient to the ER with a direct communication.

faqs.title: When anemia is part of a bigger picture

items.question: The CBC shows anemia plus thrombocytopenia. What does that mean?
items.answer_html:
Bicytopenia (two cell lines low) is a more concerning finding than isolated anemia. The differential includes: bone marrow failure (aplastic anemia), infiltrative bone marrow process (hematologic malignancy, metastatic solid tumor), hypersplenism, autoimmune destruction of multiple cell lines, B12/folate deficiency (can cause pancytopenia), or medications (chemotherapy, immunosuppressants, some antibiotics).

This requires hematology e-consult — not after a prolonged workup, but promptly.

items.question: The differential mentions "atypical lymphocytes." How concerned should I be?
items.answer_html:
Context matters. Atypical lymphocytes are common in viral infections — EBV (infectious mononucleosis), CMV, and other viral syndromes produce reactive atypical lymphocytes that are benign and self-limited. In a young patient with fever, pharyngitis, and lymphadenopathy, check monospot and EBV titers.

In an older patient without a viral prodrome, persistent atypical lymphocytes or a lymphocytosis that doesn't resolve warrants hematology e-consult to evaluate for lymphoproliferative disease.

items.question: The report says "blasts seen." What do I do?
items.answer_html:
Contact hematology today — do not wait for an e-consult response. Blasts on a peripheral smear suggest acute leukemia until proven otherwise. This is a same-day phone call to hematology, not a portal message.

While arranging urgent evaluation, do not start any new medications that could affect the marrow picture. Be direct with the patient that you have found something that requires urgent specialist evaluation.

faqs.title: Not responding to oral iron — what to do next

items.question: How do I know if the oral iron trial has been adequate?
items.answer_html:
An adequate trial requires: correct diagnosis (confirmed iron deficiency, not anemia of chronic disease), correct dose (ferrous sulfate 325 mg on alternate days or equivalent), correct administration (empty stomach, with vitamin C, away from PPIs and calcium), and sufficient duration (minimum 4 weeks for reticulocyte response, 8–12 weeks for meaningful hemoglobin rise).

Before calling a trial a failure, verify all four. The most common reason for apparent treatment failure is PPI use that was not addressed.

items.question: I've confirmed adequate trial and there's been no response. What are the possibilities?
items.answer_html:
Ongoing blood loss exceeding replacement — the source has not been identified or is continuing. Malabsorption — celiac disease, gastric bypass, inflammatory bowel disease, H. pylori gastritis. Incorrect diagnosis — reconsider whether this is truly iron deficiency or functional iron deficiency in the context of inflammation.

H. pylori infection specifically is associated with refractory iron deficiency anemia and should be tested if not already done (urea breath test or stool antigen).

items.question: When is IV iron the right answer for refractory cases?
items.answer_html:
IV iron is appropriate when oral iron has genuinely failed after an adequate trial, when malabsorption is confirmed or strongly suspected, when the patient cannot tolerate oral iron, or when rapid repletion is needed (preoperative optimization, severe symptomatic anemia).

IV iron does not require hematology referral — it can be ordered by primary care and administered in an infusion center. Refractory iron deficiency that fails IV iron, or iron deficiency without an identified source in a patient who has had appropriate GI evaluation, is the threshold for hematology e-consult.

faqs.title: Renal anemia — coordination with the CKD module

items.question: How is anemia of CKD different from other anemias?
items.answer_html:
Anemia of CKD is normocytic and normochromic, caused primarily by reduced erythropoietin production by the damaged kidneys. It typically becomes clinically apparent at eGFR < 45 (G3b) and is nearly universal at G4–G5.

It is a diagnosis of exclusion in the context of CKD — meaning iron deficiency, B12 deficiency, and other causes should be ruled out before attributing anemia solely to CKD. Many CKD patients have coexisting iron deficiency that is treatable and should be corrected before considering erythropoiesis-stimulating agents.

items.question: My CKD patient has anemia. What workup do I do?
items.answer_html:
Complete the standard Meridian anemia workup first — CBC with differential, reticulocyte count, iron studies, B12, folate. Additionally order PTH, phosphorus, and 25-OH vitamin D if not recently done as part of CKD mineral metabolism assessment.

Then cross-reference with the Meridian CKD module for integrated management. The CKD e-consult to nephrology should include the anemia workup so the nephrologist can advise on ESA candidacy with complete information.

items.question: Should I start an ESA (erythropoiesis-stimulating agent)?
items.answer_html:
ESA initiation is a nephrology decision in the primary care context. Your role is to identify the anemia, complete the workup, correct any iron deficiency (IV iron preferred in CKD — oral iron is poorly absorbed in CKD patients and worsens constipation), and document.

Do not initiate ESA therapy without nephrology input — ESAs carry risks including accelerated cardiovascular events and increased tumor progression risk in malignancy, and dosing requires monitoring that is typically managed by nephrology.

modules.default_title: Abdominal Pain — Can I discharge this patient?
modules.landing_intro: A patient presents to urgent care with abdominal pain. Our job — in 15–20 minutes — is to decide between safe discharge, close-interval re-evaluation, or ER transfer. This checklist supports that decision after a focused H&P and any reasonable rapid testing. ER referral with a reasonable workup is always appropriate and is never penalized. The only penalty is no workup at all.
modules.checklist_section_label: Before you discharge — verify all four

checklist.statement: Vitals and clinical appearance are reassuring — no fever > 101.3°F, not persistently tachycardic, tolerates oral intake, ambulates, and pain is controlled on oral analgesia
No peritoneal signs on exam — no rebound, involuntary guarding, rigidity, marked distention, or pulsatile mass
Labs, if drawn, are reassuring — no severe leukocytosis or left shift, CRP < 50 mg/L, lipase < 3× upper limit of normal — or labs were not clinically indicated
Imaging, if performed, shows no findings requiring urgent intervention — or imaging was not clinically indicated for this presentation

green_zone.zone_label: All four boxes checked — Discharge with ER precautions and 24-hour re-evaluation
green_zone.narrative_html:
Disposition supported: discharge home with ER-precautions return instructions and a scheduled re-evaluation within 24–30 hours (in-house PCP visit, structured telephone callback, or a planned UC return). The follow-up is not optional — it's the safety net that makes same-visit discharge defensible. Roughly 20–25% of non-specific abdominal pain patients have a diagnostic change at re-evaluation (Boendermaker 2018), and that structured return is what catches the ones the first visit missed.

Explicit return precautions — written and read back: worsening or migrating pain, vomiting blood, bloody or black stools, new fever, inability to tolerate fluids, fainting, or any change in mental status. Any of these → ER, not back to UC.

Documentation: use the SmartPhrase below — it captures the four-point rubric, the negatives, the return precautions, and the follow-up arrangement. ER referral with a reasonable workup is always appropriate. The only penalty is no workup.

modules.escalation_section_label: Transfer to ER or call surgeon immediately if any apply

escalation.statement: Hemodynamic instability — hypotension, persistent tachycardia despite fluids, altered mental status, or signs of sepsis
Peritoneal signs or rigid abdomen — suspected perforation, peritonitis, or ischemic bowel
Pain out of proportion to exam — especially in elderly, atrial fibrillation, or post-prandial patterns (mesenteric ischemia)
Sudden-onset excruciating pain — perforation, AAA rupture, aortic dissection, ovarian or testicular torsion

context_strip.label: Urgent care throughput
context_strip.text: Target visit 15–20 minutes. Current average ~30. ER referral with a reasonable workup is never penalized — over-workup is the hidden cost.

modules.footer_note: First-draft v1 — dedicated sub-modules for pregnancy / women of childbearing age, pediatric, and geriatric abdominal pain are planned. Iteration welcomed.

faqs.title: What 'reassuring' actually means — and why pediatrics is different

items.question: What counts as reassuring vitals and appearance?
items.answer_html:
Temperature: no fever > 101.3°F (38.5°C). A low-grade temp in a well-appearing adult is not a discharge-blocker on its own, but document and factor it in.

Heart rate: not persistently tachycardic after analgesia and fluids. Pain and anxiety drive HR; a tachycardia that does not respond to a reasonable intervention is a yellow flag.

Blood pressure: at baseline. Normotension with the patient's known baseline counts.

Appearance: tolerating oral intake, ambulating, pain controlled on oral analgesia. The patient who can't sit up for a pretzel and a sip of water is not going home on oral ibuprofen.

The underlying principle is trajectory: the patient you're about to discharge should look better than they did walking in, not the same.

items.question: A single borderline vital — does it flip the decision?
items.answer_html:
Not on its own. One isolated vital needs a reason and a recheck:

HR 105 on arrival — recheck after analgesia + 500 mL NS. If it normalizes, that's a pain/volume signal, not a disease signal.

Temp 100.9°F — combine with the rest of the picture. Febrile + RLQ pain + elevated WBC is a different patient than febrile + URI + diffuse abdominal discomfort.

Persistent tachycardia despite intervention — move toward the red zone. This is the signal that shouldn't be ignored.

Document the recheck. A single captured vital without a second look is a malpractice exposure, not a clinical decision.

items.question: What about children? (stub — dedicated pediatric module planned)
items.answer_html:
The adult rubric does not apply directly to children. A pediatric abdominal pain module is on the roadmap. Key differences to keep in mind in the meantime:

• Pediatric vitals are age-dependent — a HR of 140 is different at 2 years old than at 20. Use age-normed tables, not adult ranges.

• Classic pediatric red flags: bilious emesis (any age → surgical until proven otherwise), the intussusception triad (intermittent colicky pain, vomiting, currant-jelly stools — though most cases don't show the full triad), testicular exam in any boy with abdominal pain (torsion presents as abdominal pain), Henoch-Schönlein purpura with palpable purpura + abdominal pain.

• Dehydration assessment weighs heavier than in adults — capillary refill, mucous membranes, urine output, activity level.

• Lower threshold for PCP/ED handoff. If the in-house pediatrician is available, that's your partner; if not, a warm handoff to ED is the safer path in v1.

faqs.title: Reading the abdomen — guarding, rigidity, and what the cough test tells you

items.question: How do I differentiate voluntary from involuntary guarding?
items.answer_html:
Voluntary guarding softens with distraction. Talk to the patient, ask about their kids, bend their knees, re-approach the exam. If the abdominal wall relaxes when they're not thinking about it, it's voluntary — that's pain avoidance, not peritonitis.

Involuntary guarding does not relax. The abdominal wall stays tight regardless of distraction or positioning. That's a reflex arc from an irritated peritoneum — it's a sign, not a behavior.

Rigidity is the extreme end: a board-like abdomen that doesn't move with respiration. That patient is not going home — they're going to the ER or to your in-house surgeon.

items.question: What's the cough test, and why does it matter for urgent care?
items.answer_html:
Ask the patient to cough, or to jump/hop if they can tolerate it. Reproduction of focal abdominal pain with cough or jar is a reliable indicator of peritoneal irritation — it's the cheapest, fastest peritoneal-signs screen available and does not require you to touch the abdomen.

In a time-pressured UC environment, it's an efficient triage tool. A patient who jumps off the exam table cleanly is not peritonitic. A patient who winces and grabs their side with cough deserves a closer look.

items.question: Elderly and immunocompromised patients don't always mount a classic exam — what then?
items.answer_html:
Lower your threshold. Elderly, diabetic, and immunocompromised patients can have surgical abdomens without fever, without marked tenderness, and without classic guarding. The literature is consistent: atypical presentations are the norm, not the exception.

If the patient is age > 65, immunocompromised, on chronic steroids, or significantly obese, a normal-looking exam is less reassuring than it would be in a 30-year-old. Weight imaging more heavily, lower the bar for ER transfer, and do not let an unimpressive exam override the rest of the clinical picture.

See the elderly/immunocompromised stub under 'Pain out of proportion to exam' for more detail.

faqs.title: What to order, what the thresholds mean, and the pregnancy question

items.question: When are labs actually required vs. optional?
items.answer_html:
Labs are a tool, not a ritual. A well-appearing patient with a benign exam and a clear benign etiology (gastroenteritis that's improving, musculoskeletal wall tenderness, constipation with relief after bowel movement) does not automatically need a CBC + BMP + lipase + CRP. Order labs when they will change disposition, not to paper-chart a workup.

Order labs when:

• The diagnosis is uncertain and you're entertaining a surgical or infectious cause.

• The patient is in a higher-risk group (elderly, immunocompromised, diabetic) where the exam may underestimate severity.

• You need a negative result to support a discharge decision that would otherwise feel borderline.

Consider skipping labs when:

• The history and exam point convincingly to a benign etiology.

• The patient is well-appearing and the clinical trajectory is improving during observation.

A documented reasonable workup does not require every box checked — it requires the right boxes for this patient.

items.question: Where do the CRP < 50 and lipase < 3× ULN thresholds come from?
items.answer_html:
CRP < 50 mg/L: derived from emergency-medicine literature on non-specific abdominal pain discharge safety — values above 50 correlate with surgical pathology at meaningfully higher rates. This is an institution-calibrated number and should be treated as a decision aid, not a rule. A CRP of 55 in a well-appearing patient with an otherwise benign workup is different from a CRP of 55 with peritoneal signs.

Lipase < 3× ULN: the standard cutoff for excluding acute pancreatitis. Elevations in the 1–3× range can reflect non-specific inflammation, renal dysfunction, or other causes; > 3× is the action threshold. Pair with clinical picture — a painful patient with lipase 2.5× ULN still deserves a conversation with imaging or surgery.

Caveat: these thresholds are derived from adult, non-pregnant, general UC/ED populations. Special populations need different calibration.

items.question: Pregnancy / women of childbearing age — urine β-hCG vs. serum (stub — dedicated module planned)
items.answer_html:
Urine β-hCG is the workhorse in UC. Results in minutes, adequate sensitivity for the discharge-decision timeframe. A negative urine β-hCG in a woman of childbearing age with lower abdominal pain is generally sufficient to proceed down the general-adult discharge rubric, provided the rest of the picture is reassuring.

Serum quantitative β-hCG is orderable in-house, but turnaround is measured in hours. If ordered later in the day it may not result until the next day — it is not a real-time UC decision aid. Use serum when:

• Urine is positive and you need viability / ectopic workup (pair with pelvic US).

• Urine is negative but clinical suspicion for very early pregnancy is high enough that the sensitivity gap matters (i.e., you need a number, not a yes/no).

Document: what you ordered, the result, and whether the turnaround was compatible with a same-visit disposition. If you ordered serum late in the day knowing it wouldn't return, that needs a management plan (recheck tomorrow, ED referral, or scheduled return).

Ectopic must be actively considered in any positive-pregnancy woman with lower abdominal pain — in-house OB/GYN consult or ER transfer before discharge. A pregnant patient with abdominal pain is not a general-adult discharge case.

faqs.title: When to image, when not to, and how to document the choice

items.question: When is imaging actually indicated?
items.answer_html:
Imaging is indicated when the result will change disposition. Common UC scenarios where it will:

• RLQ pain with any concern for appendicitis — US first in children and thin adults; CT if US non-diagnostic or patient is obese.

• Flank pain with hematuria — non-contrast CT for stone evaluation.

• RUQ pain with Murphy's sign — US for cholecystitis.

• Suspected AAA — bedside US if available, CT with contrast otherwise.

• Post-operative or known-anatomy patients (hernias, prior bowel surgery) with pain — imaging nearly always indicated.

• Any patient where your gestalt is unsure and the patient is in a higher-risk group — elderly, immunocompromised, diabetic.

items.question: How do I document the rationale for NOT imaging?
items.answer_html:
The documentation for not imaging is as important as the documentation for imaging. Record:

• The differential you considered.

• The exam and lab findings that made a surgical or life-threatening cause unlikely.

• The specific return precautions given.

• The follow-up arrangement (who, when, how).

Example phrasing: "Considered appendicitis, cholecystitis, and diverticulitis; given benign exam without peritoneal signs, reassuring labs, and improvement with observation, imaging deferred. Patient instructed to return immediately for worsening pain, fever, vomiting, or inability to tolerate fluids; scheduled 24-hour phone re-evaluation."

A documented thought process is a defense. A sparse note is not.

items.question: What about the throughput cost of CT?
items.answer_html:
A same-visit CT with contrast in UC typically adds 45–90 minutes to the visit (IV placement, labs for creatinine if not already drawn, the scan itself, the read, and the clinician review). That's the difference between a 20-minute visit and a 90-minute visit.

CT is the right call when it will change disposition. It is the wrong call when it's being ordered to generate a negative result that wasn't going to change your plan anyway. 'Defensive imaging' is expensive — in time, in radiation, in patient throughput — and the cumulative cost at the clinic level is real.

If the question is 'do I need imaging to safely discharge,' the right answer is often a thoughtful re-exam and a scheduled re-evaluation rather than a scan.

faqs.title: Hemodynamic instability — logistics and stabilization

items.question: What are the logistics of ER transfer from UC?
items.answer_html:
Call EMS early. Don't wait for the patient to decompensate further. Once you've identified instability, the clock starts and the next task is transfer, not workup.

While EMS is en route: maintain IV access (two large-bore if possible), start fluid resuscitation, place on monitors (pulse ox, BP cycling, rhythm if available), obtain a focused last-round of vitals and a point-of-care glucose, and start the transfer documentation.

Call the receiving ER with a concise handoff: demographics, presentation, vitals trend, what you've done, what they're walking into. A 90-second phone call saves 30 minutes on the other end.

items.question: What can I actually do in UC before EMS arrives?
items.answer_html:
Available in most UCs: IV fluids (NS or LR boluses), supplemental O₂, broad-spectrum antibiotics if sepsis is the concern (don't wait for the ED to draw cultures — one dose started now is better than three hours later), analgesia appropriate to the picture, anti-emetics.

Not available: blood products, vasopressors, ICU-level monitoring, operative intervention, prolonged observation. If the patient needs any of those, the transfer is already the correct call.

faqs.title: Peritoneal signs — ER vs. in-house surgical path

items.question: Do I call our in-house general surgeon or send straight to the ER?
items.answer_html:
If the patient is unstable: ER. The in-house surgeon does not have ICU, anesthesia, or OR resources in our UC footprint.

If the patient is stable with clear surgical pathology (e.g., appendicitis on imaging, well-appearing, tolerating oral intake): a direct-to-surgeon conversation is reasonable and often faster. The surgeon can decide between same-day outpatient OR, ER admission via their service, or scheduled next-day surgery.

Err toward the ER when the picture is unclear or the patient's trajectory is uncertain. A surgical consult pathway is not a substitute for ED-level resources.

items.question: What can our in-house surgical capability actually do?
items.answer_html:
Available in-house: outpatient endoscopy, outpatient OR for elective or same-day straightforward cases, specialty consults (GI, urology, gynecology, general surgery) for same-day phone or in-person evaluation.

Not available in-house: cath lab, IR, ICU, regular hospital beds for observation or routine medical admission, blood banking, anesthesia support for emergent cases.

Translation: anything that needs admission, blood products, interventional radiology, or ICU-level monitoring goes to the ER regardless of how tightly coupled the surgical consult is.

faqs.title: Pain out of proportion — the one we miss

items.question: Why is mesenteric ischemia the classic 'miss' in abdominal pain?
items.answer_html:
